g.data-narration: Welcome to The Software Process - the foundation of successful software engineering.
g.text: A Structured Approach to Software Development

g.data-narration: A software process is a structured set of activities that transforms user requirements into a working software product.
g.text: A structured set of activities that transforms
user requirements into a working software product.

g.data-narration: In the 1960s-70s, the software crisis caused projects to exceed budgets, miss deadlines, and produce poor quality software.
g.text: and producing poor-quality software led to the
emergence of software engineering as a discipline.

g.data-narration: Software process models provide guidance on which activities to perform. The main models include Waterfall, Incremental, Spiral, Agile, and V-Model.
The Waterfall model follows a sequential approach where each phase must be completed before the next begins. Best for stable requirements.
The Incremental model delivers software in small increments. Each increment adds functionality, allowing early delivery and feedback.
The Spiral model combines iterative development with systematic risk analysis. Each spiral cycle includes planning, risk analysis, engineering, and evaluation.
Agile emphasizes flexibility, collaboration, and iterative development. Teams work in short sprints (2-4 weeks) delivering working software frequently.
The V-Model extends Waterfall with parallel testing activities. Each development phase has a corresponding testing phase for early error detection.
The five fundamental software process activities are: Specification, Design, Implementation, Verification, and Maintenance.
Specification gathers requirements - understanding what the software should do. Design transforms requirements into a blueprint for implementation.
Implementation transforms design into executable code. Verification ensures correctness through various testing levels.
Maintenance encompasses all activities after deployment - bug fixing, optimization, and new features. It consumes 50-80% of total lifecycle cost.
CMMI is a process improvement framework with five maturity levels: Initial, Repeatable, Defined, Quantitatively Managed, and Optimizing.
Verification answers 'Are we building the product right?' while Validation answers 'Are we building the right product?' - a key exam distinction.
To summarize: Software processes transform requirements into products through structured activities. Choose the right model based on project needs.
g.text: • Software process = structured activities from requirements to maintenance
• Choose model based on: requirements stability, project size, risk, time-to-market
• Iterative models: Agile, Spiral, Incremental | Sequential: Waterfall, V-Model
• Maintenance = 50-80% of lifecycle cost | Verification vs Validation distinction
